title: Product Emergency Process — Premium Stakeholder Edition
description: A 20-minute, premium walkthrough of how Moonfare handles product emergencies. Built for stakeholders who need to know what to expect, who does what, and how to respond — without becoming an incident responder.
author: Moonfare Product Operations

landingPage.tagline: When something breaks, this is what happens next.

modules.title: Module 1 — What Counts as an Emergency
modules.description: The threshold framework: when a product issue becomes a Moonfare emergency.

lessons.title: Recognise the signal
lessons.description: Severity classification, threshold criteria, and what the highest tier looks like.

slides.title: When something breaks

content.content:
When something breaks at Moonfare,
the response is fast,
structured, and transparent.
This 20-minute course teaches you what an emergency looks like, who owns the response, and exactly where you fit in.

slides.notes: Cover slide — sets the tone. Premium navy hero.

slides.title: The Emergency threshold

content.content: An issue becomes an Emergency when 100% of users are affected, financial impact exceeds €100,000, or there is a confirmed data breach. Anything below those triggers is a different tier.
If you spot one of these signals, escalate to #emergency_stakeholders immediately. False positives are fine — silence isn't.

slides.notes: Number-lead style slide. Anchors the 100% / €100k / data-breach triggers.

slides.title: Five tiers, one ladder

content.content: Every issue is classified at intake. The tier sets the response cadence and the people who get pulled in.

slides.notes: 3-column comparison covering 5 tiers (collapsing High/Medium/Low into one column for clarity).

slides.title: Knowledge check — Threshold

questions.text: A bug affects 5% of users and has €30,000 of impact. What tier is this?

options.text: High / Medium / Low — standard incident process

questions.explanation: Both triggers (% users and €) are below the Emergency threshold and the segment is small. This is a standard incident, not an Emergency.

questions.text: A confirmed data breach is always classified as an Emergency, regardless of how many users are affected.
questions.explanation: Data breach is one of three independent Emergency triggers. User-count is irrelevant once a breach is confirmed.

slides.notes: Module 1 knowledge check — 2 questions on threshold and classification.

modules.title: Module 2 — Who Owns the Response
modules.description: Three response roles: L1 Liaison, Senior SMEs, Command Staff. What each one does.

lessons.title: The response structure
lessons.description: How responsibility flows from intake to executive sign-off.

slides.title: Why structure matters

data.pqQuote: In an emergency, ambiguity is the enemy. Everyone needs to know — within sixty seconds — whether they're driving, supporting, or watching.

slides.notes: Pull-quote opening for Module 2 — frames the importance of role clarity.

content.content: Every Moonfare emergency is staffed by three role types working in parallel. None of them is optional.

data.cmpTitle: From front-line intake to executive ownership

bullets.text: First point of contact for stakeholders
Pulls in Command Staff if scope grows

slides.title: Role definitions

accordionItems.title: L1 Liaison
accordionItems.content: The 24/7 front line for stakeholder-reported issues. Owns triage, classification, and the public-facing cadence. Sits in Product Operations. Lily and Hasan are permanent members; PMs rotate weekly.

accordionItems.title: Senior SME (Subject Matter Expert)
accordionItems.content: The domain owner for the affected system. Elena owns Engineering. Hassan owns Security. They have authority to approve a fix without further escalation — but must pull in Command Staff if scope or impact grows.

accordionItems.title: Command Staff
accordionItems.content: Activated only for Tier-1 Emergency. Andrew (CEO), Elena, and Hassan. Owns external communication, regulator notification where applicable, and post-incident accountability. They sign off on every public statement.

accordionItems.title: Partner Relay
accordionItems.content: Max and Nicolai (Partnerships). When an emergency touches our distribution partners, all communication flows through them — never direct from response team to partner. This protects the relationship and prevents conflicting messages.

slides.notes: Glossary accordion — drill-down for each role.

slides.title: Knowledge check — Roles

questions.text: Who is responsible for posting the 30-minute Jira update during a live emergency?
questions.explanation: L1 Liaison owns the public cadence — including the 30-min Jira update — for the duration of the live response.

questions.text: When are the Command Staff (Andrew, Elena, Hassan) activated?

options.text: For every incident, regardless of tier
Only when external regulators are involved
Only after resolution, for the post-mortem

questions.explanation: Command Staff activate only at Tier-1. Lower tiers are handled by L1 Liaison plus the relevant Senior SME.

slides.notes: Module 2 quiz — 2 questions on response roles.

modules.title: Module 3 — Communication Channels
modules.description: Where updates land, how often, and the one rule about partners.

lessons.title: Channels & cadence
lessons.description: The three Slack channels, the 30-minute rule, and the partner relay.

slides.title: The 30-minute rule

content.content: For the duration of a live Tier-1 Emergency, the L1 Liaison posts an update on the Jira ticket every 30 minutes. Even when there's no progress — silence is interpreted as the response stalling.
“No update” is itself an update. A blank 30-minute window is read by stakeholders as something has gone wrong with the response — so we always post.

data.calloutTitle: Why we post even with nothing new

slides.notes: Number-lead style: anchors the 30-min cadence rule.

content.content: Where to look — and where to post

data.cmpTitle: Each one has a single audience and a single purpose

bullets.text: L1 Liaison + Senior SME + Command Staff
Post questions here, not in DMs

slides.title: The one rule about partners

content.content: When an emergency touches our distribution partners (banks, wealth managers, family offices), the natural instinct is to reach out directly. Don't. The response only works because we have one voice.
All partner-bound communication flows through Max and Nicolai in Partnerships. Never DM a partner contact directly during an active incident — even if you've worked with them for years.

data.pqQuote: Two stakeholders telling the same partner two slightly different stories is how a 90-minute incident becomes a six-month relationship problem.

slides.notes: The single hardest stakeholder rule, paired with a rationale pull-quote.

questions.text: A trusted partner contact pings you directly during an active Tier-1 Emergency. What do you do?

options.text: Reply with what you know — they need information fast
Forward to Max and Nicolai and let them respond
Wait for the next 30-minute update, then forward that

questions.explanation: Partner relay always goes through Max and Nicolai. Even with the best intentions, direct replies create message fragmentation that hurts the relationship.

questions.text: If there's nothing new to report, the L1 Liaison can skip the 30-minute update.
questions.explanation: Stakeholders read silence as the response stalling. We always post — even if the post is “no change since last update.”

modules.title: Module 4 — Lifecycle & Aftermath
modules.description: The five phases from detection to post-mortem, and the culture that makes the process work.

slides.title: The five-phase arc

content.content: From the first signal to the published post-mortem. Average wall-clock time for a contained Tier-1: 2–6 hours live, 10 working days to written post-mortem.

timelineEvents.title: Detection
timelineEvents.description: Signal arrives via monitoring, oncall page, or stakeholder report. L1 Liaison acknowledges within 5 minutes.

timelineEvents.title: Triage & Classification
timelineEvents.description: Severity tier assigned. If Tier-1, Command Staff and stakeholder channel are activated.

timelineEvents.title: Active Response
timelineEvents.description: Senior SME drives the fix. L1 Liaison posts every 30 minutes. Partner relay activates if needed.

timelineEvents.title: Resolution
timelineEvents.description: Fix verified in production, monitoring confirms recovery, stakeholder channel gets the all-clear.

timelineEvents.title: Post-mortem
timelineEvents.description: Written post-mortem published. Blameless format. Linked from the Confluence Emergencies index.

slides.notes: Full incident lifecycle as a timeline block.

data.pqQuote: Trust is built in seconds. It's lost in minutes. Our post-mortems are blameless because the next emergency is more likely to be caught by the engineer who already learned the hard way — not the one who's hiding from the fallout.

slides.title: Five things to remember

checklistItems.title: 100% users · €100k impact · data breach → Tier-1 Emergency
L1 Liaison → Senior SME → Command Staff is the escalation ladder
Updates land in #emergency_stakeholders every 30 minutes during Tier-1
Partner contact only via Max + Nicolai — never direct
Post-mortems are blameless and published within 10 working days

slides.notes: Five-point recap before the final quiz.

slides.title: Final assessment

questions.text: Within how long should the L1 Liaison acknowledge a new signal?
questions.explanation: Acknowledgement within 5 minutes is the SLA. The 30-minute number is the ongoing update cadence — different rule.

questions.text: What is the standard target for publishing a written post-mortem?
questions.explanation: 10 working days. Published in Confluence, linked from the Emergencies index, blameless.

questions.text: Moonfare uses blameless post-mortems because hiding mistakes makes the next emergency harder to catch.
questions.explanation: Exactly. Blameless culture is what makes the engineer who tripped the wire the most likely person to catch the next one.

slides.notes: Final assessment — 3 questions covering lifecycle and culture.
